text.title: Trunk-Based Feature Flags Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules every flag creation must satisfy. The bot enforces them mechanically; author prose cannot override.

rule.statement: Each flag MUST correspond to exactly one task / ticket — flag_id matches ticket_id slug. Multi-purpose flags are rejected.
rule.rationale: Multi-purpose flags grow scope drift; the cleanup work doubles every time. One-task scope keeps cleanup tractable.

rule.statement: Every flag declares owner (email, not team alias) and a target cleanup date — typical: 30 days after planned 100% rollout.
rule.rationale: Team-aliased flags have no accountable human; the flag outlives the team. Personal ownership is the only durable signal.

rule.statement: Spec lists the ramp stages with percentages and per-stage gates (e.g. 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%; gate = error_rate baseline). No flag may go to 100% in one step unless type=ops kill-switch.
rule.rationale: Single-step rollouts miss long-tail input shapes; staged ramps give the safety net the flag exists to provide.

rule.statement: Every flag has a kill-switch test: a recorded action that flips the flag off and reverts traffic to the legacy path. The test is run BEFORE first ramp-up.
rule.rationale: An untested kill-switch is not a kill-switch. The first time you need it is the worst time to discover it doesn't work.

rule.statement: Flag spec includes cleanup_ticket_id pointing to an open ticket with SLA. The cleanup ticket is filed AT flag creation, not later.
rule.rationale: Cleanup deferred is cleanup forgotten. Filing at creation is the only intervention that scales with flag count.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (flag service present, PR open, owner identifiable), execute the full spec creation: id → owner → type → ramp plan → kill-switch test → cleanup ticket. Bot blocks the PR if any field is missing.
rule.rationale: Partial specs become normal; "we'll fill it later" never happens. Atomic spec creation is the gate.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) flag service is absent and there is no plan to add one — see feature-flags-types-lifecycle first, (b) mobile/desktop release where the store is the gate, (c) one-commit feature completable same day, (d) no test coverage to catch a broken flag state.
rule.rationale: Flag overhead without flag infrastructure or test coverage degrades safety rather than improving it.
